channel.title: Halvren Notes
channel.description: Single-claim essays from the Halvren Capital research desk on operator quality, cost curves, capital culture, and the framework.

item.title: How to read a Canadian oil & gas operator in seven numbers
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Seven metrics in order: sustaining capex per barrel, free-cash break-even, net debt at strip, per-share growth, insider buys, prior-cycle dividends, replacement.

item.title: The cost curve is a lie if you let them pick the quartile
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Every operator claims first-quartile. The denominator decides. Three questions to test the claim, with examples from gold, silver, uranium, and copper.

item.title: What 2015 and 2020 told us about every Canadian energy operator still standing
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Two stress tests sorted Canadian energy operators into four buckets. The 2015 and 2020 records are public. The cohort splits the same way in 2026.

item.title: Pipelines are turnpikes, not commodity bets — reading Canadian infrastructure honestly
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Canadian and US pipeline operators earn from contracted rate base, not the commodity tape. The contracted share is the moat. Capital discipline is the rest.

item.title: The uranium operator's checklist: separating the mines from the narratives
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Six questions separate uranium producers from developers and narratives from mines. Most uranium names fail the first four. The remaining list is short.

item.title: Silver's per-share problem: why production growth isn't shareholder value
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Most senior silver producers grow absolute ounces and destroy per-share value while doing it. The arithmetic is in the proxy. The streamer model is the workaround.

item.title: The dividend that survived: 26 consecutive raises at Canadian Natural
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Twenty-six consecutive years of dividend raises at Canadian Natural, through 2015 and 2020. The record is a capital-allocation document, not a dividend story.

item.title: Insider buying vs. insider granting — the only signal that costs the operator something
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Options are loyalty to a quarter; open-market purchases are loyalty to a decade. The only insider signal that costs the executive real money, read carefully.

item.title: What boring looks like on a thirty-year chart: the case for Canadian infrastructure compounding
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: A 6% earnings compounder plus a 4% dividend yield for thirty years. Canadian infrastructure is the boring half of the desk, and the half that pays for the wait.

item.title: ROIC on incremental capital, in plain English
item.author: amirali@halvrencapital.com (Amirali Karimi)
item.description: Marginal ROIC, not average ROIC, is what the next dollar earns. The math is mechanical. Most operators do not publish it because the answer is uncomfortable.
